column3: I am a registered Electrical Engineer in seven states (NC, MO, FL, VA, TX, AR and ME). I have experience in manufacturing and engineering consulting. Engineering experience includes: electrical safety (Arc Flash), protective relaying (SEL), standby power generation systems, and commercial/higher education design. I am a graduate of the University of Arkansas where I received a Masters degree in Electrical Engineering (2019) where my coursework focused on power systems. I received my bachelors degree in Electrical Engineering from North Carolina State University in 2013 with a concentration on renewable electric energy systems. Currently working at Duke Energy.

column1:

column2: I love being able to give back to my Alma Mater but also find a lot of reward in helping others grow personally and in their career.
column3:
I have been an employee of MIT Lincoln Laboratory since I graduated from NC State in 2014.  With MIT, I have been part of several Missile Defense groups where my work has ranged from the development of Electronic Warfare systems to the improvement and modernization of operational radar systems that are part of the National Defense network.  My background is in RF hardware and Electromagnetics which has served quite well in my current position.

As part of the Electronics Research Laboratory while a graduate student at NC State, my research was on the interaction of acoustic and electromagnetic fields for remote sensing applications.  My research interests include nonlinear acoustic modeling, high powered acoustics, electromagnetic, RF system design, nonlinear RF effects, electronic warfare techniques and applications, and radar design.
column4: I am a huge sports fan and love to be active in general -- you can generally find me joining just about every sports recreation league possible and typically have a game (regardless of sport) on in the background.  I really enjoy food and finding new places with amazing food.  Food trucks are my favorite but now that I live in the middle of the Pacific, I highly enjoy visiting countries around the world where I can explore the local food cuisine.  I've been married to a designer for the past 3 years (traditional story, she was a Peace girl while I was at NC State) and the differences in how our brains work fascinate me -- or confound me, depending on the day.

Being involved in a number of small or startup companies I got experiences involving a number of other areas of specialization in EE that include the design and testing of: memories, data converters, DSP, SoC, FPGAs, ASICs, Wireless/RF, Software, Firmware, Digital Communications and 2D & 3D video technologies. However, more resently, I am circling back to uP roots, I have now developed novel A.I. and Machine Learning (ML) technologies as well as technologies relating to High Performance Computing (HPC) of which both A.I. & ML are just a small subset of HPC!

column1: Throughout my life I have been a part of both formal and informal mentorship programs as both mentor and mentee.  Most recently at ABB, I am mentor to a handful of junior colleagues based in the US.
column2:
The education and development provided to me by NC State is invaluable, but without the connection points to the industry through the alumni network, I might have never charted my current path.  

As a student, I am thankful for the alumni I interacted with who were, and continue to be excited to share perspective and advice.  As a mentor, I hope to continue this feedback loop of support.

Additionally, as I have become a few years removed from my undergraduate experience, there is nothing more energizing/exciting than speaking to the next generation of students.  I'd be lying if I didn't admit to learning something new every chance I get to connect with students.
column3:
After graduating from NC State, I joined ABB's US rotational development program, where breadth of exposure and fast transitions were to build my internal network and understanding of how such a large international business operates successfully.  My assignments were focused on government affairs, technical sales, and engineering.  After completing the program, I joined ABB's US Business Development team, supporting regional strategy and merger & acquisition activities.  

Since last year, I've taken a global strategy role, as Chief of staff to the President for ABB's Electrification Business Area based at ABB HQ in Zurich Switzerland.  As an advisor to the business leadership team, I contribute to aligning our focus and identifying growth opportunities for our $13 billion business focused in low and medium voltage electrification and building control solutions.

Though these roles have not always been the most technical in nature, I couldn't do what I do at ABB without a strong background in electrical engineering.  It is impossible to manage a business without a strong understanding of how we support our customers.  As such, a large number of our business leadership have strong technical backgrounds.

Also worked the ECE Summer camp Jul’13 under Dr R. Gupta, walking highschool kids the old Carlson ECE306 project(infrared RC car tracks). Soldering, basic, testing/debugging..
column2:
I remember what it felt like being stuck in school year 2-3.. without any context on what lies on the other side. It felt pretty disorienting, I nearly quit several times. I stuck with it, and found daylight on the other side. I want to reassure someone that might be near that tipping point. ECE is a tough very broad curriculum, and that it all just needs to be put into perspective. Especially with the last ~18 mo of COVID, I dont know if 2010-me would have stuck with it.. So, I’d like to lend a hand, help give someone perspective/ motivation, and do my part. 

When I have an intern, it’s not about what they can give to our company.. it’s about how I can help them grow their resume/skillset/confidence. I’m.. assuming.. that I would treat this similarly, except that I might need some additional details on the process/workflow.
column3:
Started off in QA automation (Sensus, 2014), C# development, embedded product testing. Verifying “smart meter” (smart grid) behaviors/ systems. Specialized on electric meters. Talking.. modulations/ bit compression / frequencies/ binary messaging protocol encoding/decoding. Automated entire lab environments using raspberry pi hosts, mobile/web automation as well.

Moved to Engineering product team May 2017, Senior Software Engineer, rewriting legacy C code into .NET/C#, again targeting binary messaging/embedded device support, but leading the customer software side. Had teammates in NC, Louisiana, Bangalore India.. got to visit them all & create/grow a team where there previously wasnt one.. Great experience

Learning of the generational gap that existed ( and still does to this day) in the power industry, I realized that it would make a fulfilling and stable career. Post graduation, I went from ABB to Dashiell to help start up a new division office for the Houston based, traditionally oil and gas company. At the Cary office of Dashiell we began to pursue renewable interconnection projects and built the first CAISO interconnected solar project in 2009, a whopping 5MW project! We went on to interconnect over 500MW of projects during my tenure at Dashiell (and they have continued to grow since my departure). Renewables became a major aspect of a traditionally oil and gas company and that excited me for the future. I made the move from Dashiell to Swinerton Renewable Energy (now SOLV Energy) in 2014 when I opened an office in downtown Raleigh. Three of the first 4 employees were classmates of mine in Electrical Engineering at NCSU. Since joining SOLV Energy, we have grown to be the #1 EPC and O&M provider for utility scale solar projects in the US. Our group has now interconnected over 6GW of solar projects and manages nearly 7GW of solar projects from our 24/7 Operation and Control Center at our headquarters in San Diego, CA.

I was later hired on full-time as a substation designer. Throughout my 3 years there, I progressed from the designer to a project engineer and then to a project manager. I then joined SEL as an Application Engineer. I designed, programmed, and tested protection relays. I taught at SEL University where I educated customers on topics such as communication-based protection schemes, SEL's relay portfolio and basic three-phase power fundamentals. 

I now work at Pike Engineering as a Engineer III. We provide design and construction services to Duke Energy for the purpose of grid hardening. A majority of the work is at the distribution level and mainly in downtown Raleigh. It involves calculating, designing, and routing power supply to the multitude of new construction sites that are popping up around the city.

column1:
I've been a mentee during all of my jobs so far. My first mentor in civil engineering helped me discover that I didn't like working in civil engineering and to appreciate the monolith of government regulations in buildings and medical fields. When I first moved into college, I met my second professional mentor, an old friend from high school who was the news editor at the NC State technician. He encouraged me to try journalism and helped me interview all sorts of people, writing about critical issues like the 2016 election, housing inequality at NC State, tuition and fees at NC State, and racial/accessibility issues on campus in 2016 and 2017. He made me feel important in the news room and made excellent software systems for reading in news ideas. After the newspaper, I was a mentee at my summer internship at Plexus corp. My mentor was an entry-level software engineer that had recently joined the company. He helped give me excellent feedback on my project ideas and helped guide me toward literature to help me learn more about production-scale software development philosophies. I still have a mentor from Plexus who lives in Colorado who was the senior engineer and SCRUM master for the robotic surgeon testing suite I developed there. He gives me career opportunities when he finds them, gives me feedback on my work in Raleigh, and we enjoy playing board games together. 

At NC State, my coursework and research led to at least a dozen mentee opportunities (I cannot write about them all): I was first a mentee in ECE 200 and ECE 309, the car class under my TAs John and Brody. Brody gave me excellent feedback on my programming approach and helped guide me toward becoming an intern at Plexus. He also helped explain the intricacies of FDA clearance and approval so that I could be a better intern there. John helped me appreciate my problem solving fluency in mathematics and to pursue higher-level mathematics in control theory and analysis at NC State. I really enjoyed talking through physics problems with him. I was an entrepreneurial mentor and mentee in the NC State entrepreneurship program, receiving project advice on a robotic stove knob from Marshall Brain II. Marshall gave my team good feedback on the practicality of our inventions and helped connect us to manufacturing methods and design concerns, even when we felt completely lost. In my research, I was mentored by Dr. Bitzer, the inventor of the plasma screen. He shared his love for intuitive problem solving with me and helped me feel appreciated and confident working with extremely complex patents and unused software, even when I didn't know the way forward and needed room to grow. Lastly, I was mentored by Dr. Kolbas at NC State during his ECE 303 class and afterward when I made a make-a-thon event for my department. Dr. Kolbas directed my passion for helping other students learn invention skills and helped me to become more humble and curious around simple physics problems. I remember him telling me that it didn't matter what math I would need to learn in the future if I weren't curious now about simple things, like ice, clouds, arcs, and real-world discrepancies compared to my models.

I've been a mentor to college students in two different ways: as a TA in ECE 200, and as the creator of a one-time make-a-thon for engineering students. I was a TA on 3 separate occasions in my 3.5 undergrad years at NC State, and my philosophy was that student names and listening were far more important than solely delivering course content. From our first classes, I made an effort to greet every single student and to ensure that they knew they could talk to me about anything. Some students needed help navigating the ECE program's career outcomes and internships while others needed help understanding mathematical concepts. I hope that in my time talking with them, I was able to help them become more successful and to contribute to positive engineering outcomes -- some of them still run into me at the grocery store, and some of them have been published by NC State's engineering magazine for their inventions. In my make-a-thon, I was mentored by Elizabeth in the ECE undergrad office. She helped me understand how to organize my project to be successful and helped me understand my aversions to deadlines and feedback better. She helped me become more vulnerable and to host 3 electrical engineering projects and a contest day for other students using funds from Dr. Kolbas. I mentored 3 project groups since I was unable to find any willing ECE student mentors: I worked with the groups to develop their social media presence, development process, and to feel comfortable with failure when some of their inventions were more difficult or complicated than planned.

Lastly, I've been a mentor to younger children in four ways: a tutoring program, a literacy program, a soldering skills program, and as a member of the radio club at NC State. I was a tutor to Sudanese immigrants in Greensboro, NC for 6 years while I was in middle and high school. I helped provide them with free after-school math tutoring and out-loud reading -- the reading part being especially difficult. In high school, I was a mentor in a program called Brooks Buddies where I worked with a child for 4 years weekly on Tuesdays or Thursdays -- I'd listen and learn from him talking about life and his family, teach him about my experiences, and read books with him or talk about books that we were reading to help him develop his literacy skills and love for reading. I was a mentor in Girls Engineering Change to elementary, middle, and high school girls. I discussed careers in electrical engineering with them, drew simplified circuit diagrams with them, and made them feel comfortable working with electronics and soldering. When I was in the radio club, I helped host a Boy Scout radio merit badge day where I taught antenna physics and radio uses to troops around the triangle area. I got to use my homemade yogi antenna with them and to demystify electrodynamics for a young audience.
column2: I'm interested in helping make a community of mentorship for ECE students so that they feel less alone and more supported in this job market. It's really difficult starting out in a remote job for me, and I think that connecting with students will give them the skills and support they need to make amazing contributions in whatever they do.
column3:
My fields of interest are analysis, statistics, machine learning, control theory, electrodynamics, circuit design, sensing, embedded software, networking, and advanced computing. My main interest is improving the utility of existing technology and helping technology adapt to environmental and medical applications. 

I'm currently an embedded engineer for Signalscape, Inc. developing our Android software portfolio. I created a training site for new engineers, our first successful Android application, and am working on a release for our next Android application next year. I've also integrated a cellphone protocol into an existing embedded device so that it could make phone calls. My favorite part of this job has been hosting a weekly coffee club where I organize a team of barista-engineers to showcase locally roasted single-origin coffees. I'm also a member of the company social committee where I've organized a high-ropes outing, a company dinner, an indoor put-put, a St. Patricks Day trivia, and other events during the pandemic.

I've been an embedded engineer for 2 years for Plexus (Raleigh) on a robotic surgeon prototype to compete with the DaVinci robot. I made a testing suite for it that formed a network between a hardware prototype, an electrical stimulator, and a Windows computer. 

Before I was an embedded engineer, I was a journalist for 2 years at the NC State technician and a bioinformatics research assistant at NC State's department of computer science. I managed months-long investigations into issues of equity, diversity, and accessibility and interviewed almost everyone in campus administration at least once. I was a private tutor in ECE subjects as well during this time as well as an ECE TA for 3 semesters.
column4:
I play piano almost daily, Rachmaninoff, Scriabin, religious music, and Debussy are current interests. I perform in worship services and at public pianos when I can find them, and in the next year, I may join a local group to perform with. I've played for 16 years now. I also play guitar and trombone but haven't performed with them since the pandemic.

I love to ride my bike and run around the Kentwood neighborhood and the Raleigh Greenway. I also do indoor FTP training when the weather is bad. I do calisthenics, hiking, walking, and backpacking as well.

I'm a big board game nerd: I host a board game night with my friends weekly and really enjoy complex, thematic strategy games like Spirit Island and nuanced classic card games like karma and canasta.

I love traveling to historical and natural areas. During the first months of the pandemic, I explored all of the county parks in Wake County. My recent trips include the Joyce Kilmer memorial forest, the Grayson Highlands, Fort Fisher, and Monticello.

Sometimes, when I find a new codebase, I may open it up and modify it. I recently discovered an abandoned Android application that selectively disables a pixel array on the AMOLED screen on my Google Pixel phone.

column2: My postdoc advisor (Dr. G Allan Johnson) was especially helpful during the time I was figuring out what I was going to do with my PhD after school and the whole training to become more statically minded. I've really enjoyed the opportunity to work with students both at Duke and NCSU.  I would love to be able to help a student in a similar way that is not directly working with them for research tasks (which was mainly what I had done in the past).
column3:
I was really uncertain what I major I wanted when I first started in undergraduate for engineering. Engineering wise, I was between biomedical/electrical/civil as possible options when I applied. I started during the Great Recession which made the idea of doing something related to the housing industry scary. I doubted at the time I would be able to get into the biomedical program with how exclusive it was and thought electrical while it didn't have biology would cover similar areas with more generality. Initially after a convocation major information event, I wanted to do electrical engineering focused in power because the renewables concentration was very new and I liked environmental causes. But once I took the power class, it was not of interest.  

I found the E&M courses in undergrad. I did research in my undergraduate in an optics lab (ie E&M you can see) for Dr. Escuti (no longer at NC State now at Meta) who later became my graduate advisor. I worked for years as a graduate student making polarization optics components (color filters/lenses etc with liquid crystals) for various collaborators at NC State (like Dr. Kudenov), within the US, and abroad who had specialized imaging requirements.

After my PhD,  I was burned out and didn't think I could move to the west coast (where the majority of jobs for optics seemed to be at the time) for a variety of personal reasons. I applied to an imaging postdoc at Duke (as I've always been into biomedical or human centered imaging tasks) who passed my resume on to my current boss who does pre-clinical super high resolution MRI and compares it to light sheet microscopy results in an ex-vivo mouse model. He needed someone to do the statistical analysis of the MRI contrasts obtained, so I retrained to learn about statistical approaches and techniques. 

Rather than do the typical postdoc path towards academia (and leading my own lab), I've taken an approach to become a staff researcher. I became full-time staff at Duke University Medical Center in December 2021. I get to do day to day science figuring out how the brains of these mice are effected by the various diseases, and just process of aging, that they are subjected to which hopefully can inform next-generation clinical investigations.

Overall, my professional interests lie in Software engineer, Software and Network Security , Cloud Technologies, and Agile Project Management.

Formally Trained as a software engineer focusing on how software is used in networking and security applications in my time at NC State. While at NC State I held 3 internships as both a Graduate and Undergraduate student. One was an embedded/software testing internship with Plexus working on a robotical surgical system. The other two were with NetApp devloping tools such as a language parser to help diagnose problems withing cloud systems. Also while at NC state I was a Makerspace Student Ambassador where I worked to maintain the libraries fleet of 3D printers, Laser Cutter, and Raspberry Pi's. In the makerspace I also consulted with students faculty and staff around campus to provide them with the engineering knowledge they needed to complete their research or projects. 

At my current role At Lockheed martin I have worked on two teams. The first, created a software microservice biased for a Nation State secure satellite communication system. I was in charge of developing backend rest endpoints. As well as doing security sys admin work to configure firewalls and other network security measure across our servers. The second project I worked on is with the Human spaceflight devison working on software tools to build the NASA Class A flight software for the ORION capsule used in the Artemis lunar missions. On this project I audit and verify code for defects in flight software as well as devlope full stack solutions that allow other engineers to more easily meet requirements.

Besides work, I was inducted into NC State's Beta Eta chapter of IEEE Eta Kappa Nu in 2018. I Have been an active member of the organization since then, serving first as chapter advisor then as chapter president. Now as an alumni I serve as the chapter advisor and still mentor chapter leaders to this day.
